elements.sent-2411-003: The search for new quantum spin liquid materials relies on systems with strong frustration such as spins on the kagome lattice.

elements.sent-2411-005: In recent work the 2D kagome system YCu$_3$(OH)$_6$[(Cl$_x$Br$_{(1-x)}$)$_{3-y}$(OH)$_y$] has emerged as a leading candidate hosting a Dirac spin liquid which appears to survive at least for $x<0.4$, associated with alternating-bond hexagon (ABH) disorder.
elements.sent-2411-006: Here in samples with $x=0.58$ we report an unusual coexistence of anti-ferromagnetic (AFM) and ferromagnetic (FM) order, and propose a semi-microscopic model to account for this order.
elements.sent-2411-007: Combined with a phenomenological model the results suggest growth of large FM clusters at intermediate temperatures which order eventually at lower temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-008: Generating these FM clusters relies on two ingredients: ABH disorder, and local-AFM FM-canting generated by small Kitaev interactions which can arise on the kagome lattice.
elements.sent-2411-009: The combination of experimental observation and theory suggests that Kitaev interactions and ABH disorder are necessary for describing the magnetic fluctuations in this family of materials, with potential implications for the proposed proximate spin liquid phase.

elements.sent-2411-066: Before going to the details of that discussion, in this section we first give a brief review of the possible magnetic ordering in the clean kagome limit with more conventional Heisenberg and DM interaction and discuss the relevant ordering pattern for the current material.
elements.sent-2411-067: The most dominant symmetry allowed spin exchange Hamiltonian on ideal single layer kagome lattice can be written as~\cite{ElhajalPRB2002 , \begin{equation}H=J\sum_{\langle ij\rangle} S_i\cdot S_j+D_z \sum_{\langle ij\rangle }\left(S_i\times S_j\right)_z+\cdots \label{eq_heis_dm}\end{equation} where the first and the second term denote the isotropic Heisenberg and anisotropic DM interaction with DM vector being perpendicular to the kagome plane, and ``$\cdots$" denote other symmetry allowed couplings which will be discussed later.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Note that the DM coupling is allowed due to the lack of inversion symmetry about the kagome bond centre, and its strength is linearly proportional to the spin-orbit coupling ($\lambda$), $|D_{z |\propto \lambda J$~\cite{ElhajalPRB2002 .}}
elements.sent-2411-069: For the Heisenberg only term, the model is frustrated and favors a coplanar 120$^0$ AFM states on the kagome network at low temperatures via order by disorder.
elements.sent-2411-070: On the other hand, the DM term selects states with specific handedness among that subspace via a finite temperature phase transition.
elements.sent-2411-071: For $D_z>0$, the ``chiral" structures are chosen which preserve the $C_3$ rotation symmetry of the kagome lattice about the midpoint of a triangle, see Fig. \ref{fig_antichiral (b).}
elements.sent-2411-072: The symmetry acts on both the spin and spatial (spin-orbit coupled) indices of the magnetic degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-073: For $D_z<0$, ``antichiral" structures are chosen which do not have any physical $C_3$ rotation symmetry, see Fig. \ref{fig_antichiral (c).}
elements.sent-2411-074: Although they also appear at the linear order in spin-orbit coupling, they are much weaker than the out-of-plane DM term (because they arise from inter-plane effects), hence their effects on the chiral and antichiral states can be computed perturbatively.
elements.sent-2411-075: For the chiral states, the in-plane terms generate a out-of-plane canting of the spins leading to a net FM moment perpendicular to the kagome plane.
elements.sent-2411-076: The relevant material showing no out-of-plane magnetization then rules out the possibility of the chiral order.
elements.sent-2411-077: The antichiral states, on the other hand, shows no out-of-plane canting with the in-plane DM terms.
elements.sent-2411-078: This can be clearly seen from a symmetry argument: the in-plane DM vectors transform oppositely under $C_3$ rotations compared to the antichiral spin order.
elements.sent-2411-079: Thus there is no energy gain due to the DM term for canting and the cost due to the Heisenberg exchage forbids it.

elements.sent-2411-096: There are two sources of the frustration on kagome lattice: frustration inherent to the kagome geometry leading to various possibilities of competing orders, and frustration due to the competing interactions.
elements.sent-2411-097: The kagome lattice with equal nearest-neighbor couplings is frustrated already for classical Heisenberg interactions.
elements.sent-2411-098: In this scenario, the small DM coupling in addition to the Heisenberg interaction favors the antichiral states partially relieving the frustration.
elements.sent-2411-099: The ABH, on the other hand, incorporating the stronger and weaker couplings, modifies the classical energy (per bond) of the classical antichiral order from $J/2$ to $J/2 - \Delta J^2/6J$.
elements.sent-2411-100: Such energy modification is associated with the local FM canting of the spins on the disordered triangles, indicating further reduction of frustration.
elements.sent-2411-101: However, this local canting arising from ABH gives no net coarse-grained FM moment, but rather a larger-scale AFM pattern (see SI for details).
elements.sent-2411-102: The local reduction of frustration around the ABH can potentially help to set in short-range ordering on the ABH more easily than the rest of the pristine kagome, leading to the cluster model which has been introduced phenomenologically in the earlier sections.
elements.sent-2411-103: This effect is also seen when including quantum effects in exactly diagonalizing the 12-site ABH model: at larger $b/J > 0.2$, (sufficiently away from the classical ordered limit, $b/J < 0.5$) the classical spin moment $|S|_\text{avg}$ is greatly enhanced by $\Delta J$, with a positive jump arising with a level crossing with increasing $\Delta J$ (Fig. \ref{fig_quantum analysis_hexagon_spin expectation_magnetic field ).}
elements.sent-2411-104: Phenomenologically, this enhanced $|S|_\text{avg}$ can be interpreted as a local enhancement of short-range order near each ABH.
elements.sent-2411-105: An interesting corollary of the above effect can be the pinning of density of the short-ranged cluster to that of the ABH, which might be worth understanding experimentally.
elements.sent-2411-107: Now considering quantum effects in full, it is clear that ABH supports singlet formation on the strong bonds, in addition to any other effects.
elements.sent-2411-108: Such singlet formation can successfully compete against classical orders: even the most naive valence bond solid states which consist of singlets on strong bonds and free spins elsewhere on ABH enjoy an energy per bond of $J/2 - \Delta J/2$.
elements.sent-2411-109: This effect is seen in the 12-site ABH model: at small $b/J \lesssim 0.2$ the classical spin moment $|S|_\text{avg}$ is suppressed by $\Delta J$ (Fig. \ref{fig_quantum analysis_hexagon_spin expectation_magnetic field ), and at any $b$ the strong bonds support increasing quantum fluctuations in the bond energy ($\langle S_i \cdot S_j \rangle - \langle S_i \rangle \cdot \langle S_j \rangle$) with $\Delta J$ (Fig. \ref{fig_quantum analysis_fluctuation_st ).}}
elements.sent-2411-110: Phenomenologically, this enhanced quantum fluctuation can result in suppression of long-range classical order and enhanced regime of short-range order arising from ABH.
elements.sent-2411-111: Such effects are especially quite significant near the $T_N \sim 14$ K, where the short-range order is weak enough.
elements.sent-2411-112: At much lower temperatures, the mean field due to the short-ranged domains enhances, leading to long-range ordered phase at $T^* \sim 7$ K.
elements.sent-2411-113: Clearly, the quantum effect of ABH can be thought of as stretching the domain of short-ranged ordering in the material.

elements.sent-2411-120: We note that the Kitaev interaction explicitly breaks the continuous $U(1)$ symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-121: Expanding the classical Kitaev energy around the antichiral state perturbatively within a single triangle ($q=0$) analysis appropriate for this $q=0$ order, we obtain: \begin{equation}\frac{H_Kitaev}{J_Kitaev} \approx -\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{6}\left(a\cos(2\phi_1)+b\sin(2\phi_1)\right)+O(a^2,b^2,ab), \label{eq_classical energy_1}\end{equation} where $a$ and $b$ denote the in-plane deviations of the spin configuration from antichiral order: \begin{equation}\phi_2-\phi_3=\frac{2\pi}{3}+\frac{a}{\sqrt{3}}~~, ~~\phi_2+\phi_3-2\phi_1=2\pi+b, \label{eq_expansion around antichiral_ising}\end{equation} and, $\phi_1,~\phi_2,~\phi_3$ are the angel variables measured from the $x-$axis, describing any in-plane spin configuration with the indices defined in the anticlockwise manner (As an illustrative example, see the triangle formed by sites 7, 1 and 2 in bottom right inset of Fig. \ref{fig_quantum analysis_hexagon_spin expectation_magnetic field ).}
elements.sent-2411-122: Note that the leading term in the energy is a $\phi_1$ independent constant, which implies although the Kitaev term breaks the $U(1)$ explicitly, there is still an emergent $U(1)$ invariance in the antichiral subspace.
elements.sent-2411-123: The classical Kitaev energy is minimized by the following spin configurations: \begin{equation}J_Kitaev>0 ~(AFM)~: ~f_1= 2\phi_1+\pi, J_Kitaev<0 ~(FM)~: ~f_1= 2\phi_1,\end{equation} where, $f_1=\tan^{-1 (b/a)$.}
elements.sent-2411-124: Clearly, the canting configuration is tied to the overall symmetry breaking angle $\phi_1$.

elements.section-2411-007: Canting due to bond-Ising or Kitaev terms
elements.sent-2411-224: In this section, we consider two potential perturbation to the Heisenberg Hamiltonian which are bond-Ising interaction and Kitaev interaction.
elements.sent-2411-225: Both of these interactions have similar symmetry, and they break continuous $U(1)$ symmetry of the Heisenberg Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-226: Via a single triangle (${\bf q =0$) classical analysis, we show that such terms indeed favor a uniform FM canting of the underlying antichiral AFM order.}
elements.sent-2411-227: Since we are considering q=0 magnetic orders, it is enough to look at a single triangle.
elements.sent-2411-228: The bond-Ising and Kitaev terms are given by: \begin{equation}H_Ising=J_Ising\sum_{\langle ij\rangle} \left(S_i\cdot ({\bf r_i}-{\bf r_j})\right)\left(S_j\cdot ({\bf r_i}-{\bf r_j})\right) H_Kitaev=J_Kitaev\sum_{\langle ij\rangle_\mu}S_i^\mu S_j^\mu~~(\mu=X,Y,Z) X = \Big\{-\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\Big\}, ~Y = \Big\{-\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}, -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\Big\}, ~Z = \Big\{\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}, 0, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\Big\}\end{equation} where in the last line $X, Y$ and $Z$ of the Kitaev interaction are given in terms of the reference axes $(x, y, z)$ described in Fig. \ref{fig_reference .}
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}H_Ising=J_Ising\sum_{\langle ij\rangle} \left(S_i\cdot ({\bf r_i}-{\bf r_j})\right)\left(S_j\cdot ({\bf r_i}-{\bf r_j})\right) H_Kitaev=J_Kitaev\sum_{\langle ij\rangle_\mu}S_i^\mu S_j^\mu~~(\mu=X,Y,Z) X = \Big\{-\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\Big\}, ~Y = \Big\{-\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}, -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\Big\}, ~Z = \Big\{\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}, 0, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\Big\}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-229: We note that although both the terms break the continuous $U(1)$ symmetry, there is still an emergent $U(1)$ symmetry in the antichiral subspace.
elements.sent-2411-230: Here we perturbatively compute the classical energy of the above two Hamiltonian around the anti-chiral state.
elements.sent-2411-231: For this we define, \begin{equation}\theta_2-\theta_3=\frac{2\pi}{3}+\frac{a}{\sqrt{3}}~~, ~~\theta_2+\theta_3-2\theta_1=2\pi+b \label{eq_expansion around antichiral_ising}\end{equation} Note that the scaling of $a$ and $b$ is chosen such that when Eq. \ref{eq_expansion around antichiral_ising is substituted in the classical Heisenberg energy, the leading order correction becomes $\propto (a^2+b^2)$.}
elements.sent-2411-232: Solving $\theta_2$ and $\theta_3$ from the above two equations, we can substitute them in the classical energy expression for the bond-Ising and Kitaev terms.
elements.sent-2411-233: Expanding in powers of $a$ and $b$, we obtain (here we express the classical energies in the units of the corresponding echange interaction scale): \begin{equation}\frac{H_Ising}{J_{Ising}} =\frac{1}{4}\left[-2\cos\left(2\theta_1+b\right)+\sqrt{3}\cos\left(2\theta_1+\frac{b}{2}+\frac{a}{2\sqrt{3}}-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)\right.\nonumber +\cos\left(2\theta_1+\frac{b}{2}-\frac{a}{2\sqrt{3}}-\frac{2\pi}{3}\right)+2\sin\left(\frac{a}{2\sqrt{3}}-\frac{b}{2}-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+2\sin\left(\frac{a}{2\sqrt{3}}+\frac{b}{2}-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)\nonumber \left.-2\sin\left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{3}}+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+\sin\left(2\theta_1+\frac{a}{2\sqrt{3}}+\frac{b}{2}-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+\sqrt{3}\sin\left(2\theta_1-\frac{a}{2\sqrt{3}}+\frac{b}{2}+\frac{2\pi}{3}\right)\right] \approx-\frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{4} \left(a\cos(2\theta_1)+b\sin(2\theta_1)\right)+O(a^2,b^2,ab)\end{equation}.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[scale=0.4]{Supplementary_figures/Reference_frame_1.png} \caption{Reference axes for a single triangle classical analysis. Note that in the main text all the angles ($\phi_i$) are measured from vertical axis. Therefore, the two conventions are related by: $\phi_i=\theta_i+\pi/2$} \label{fig_reference}
elements.sent-2411-234: \begin{equation}\frac{H_Kitaev}{J_Kitaev} =\frac{1}{6} \left(2 \cos\left(b + 2 \theta_1\right) - \sqrt{3} \cos\left(\frac{1}{6} \left(\sqrt{3} a + 3 b - \pi + 12 \theta_1\right)\right) \right.\nonumber - \cos\left(\frac{1}{6} \left(-\sqrt{3} a + 3 b + 4 \pi + 12 \theta_1\right)\right) + 2 \sin\left(\frac{1}{6} \left(\sqrt{3} a - 3 b - \pi\right)\right) + 2 \sin\left(\frac{1}{6} \left(\sqrt{3} a + 3 b - \pi\right)\right)\nonumber \left. - 2 \sin\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} a + \frac{\pi}{6}\right) - \sin\left(\frac{1}{6} \left(\sqrt{3} a + 3 b - \pi + 12 \theta_1\right)\right) - \sqrt{3} \sin\left(\frac{1}{6} \left(-\sqrt{3} a + 3 b + 4 \pi + 12 \theta_1\right)\right)\right) \approx -\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{6}\left(a\cos(2\theta_1)+b\sin(2\theta_1)\right)+O(a^2,b^2,ab)\end{equation} Note that $\theta_1$ independent constant term is the consequence of the emergent $U(1)$ invariance in the antichiral subspace.
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}\frac{H_Kitaev}{J_Kitaev} =\frac{1}{6} \left(2 \cos\left(b + 2 \theta_1\right) - \sqrt{3} \cos\left(\frac{1}{6} \left(\sqrt{3} a + 3 b - \pi + 12 \theta_1\right)\right) \right.\nonumber - \cos\left(\frac{1}{6} \left(-\sqrt{3} a + 3 b + 4 \pi + 12 \theta_1\right)\right) + 2 \sin\left(\frac{1}{6} \left(\sqrt{3} a - 3 b - \pi\right)\right) + 2 \sin\left(\frac{1}{6} \left(\sqrt{3} a + 3 b - \pi\right)\right)\nonumber \left. - 2 \sin\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} a + \frac{\pi}{6}\right) - \sin\left(\frac{1}{6} \left(\sqrt{3} a + 3 b - \pi + 12 \theta_1\right)\right) - \sqrt{3} \sin\left(\frac{1}{6} \left(-\sqrt{3} a + 3 b + 4 \pi + 12 \theta_1\right)\right)\right) \approx -\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{6}\left(a\cos(2\theta_1)+b\sin(2\theta_1)\right)+O(a^2,b^2,ab)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-235: Substituting: \begin{equation}a=r\cos(f_1)~~, ~~b=r\sin(f_1)\end{equation} We get: \begin{equation}H_Ising/J_Ising\approx-\frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{4}r\cos(f_1-2\theta_1)+O(a^2,b^2,ab) H_Kitaev/J_Kitaev\approx-\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{6}r\cos(f_1-2\theta_1)+O(a^2,b^2,ab)\end{equation} Note that the classical energy is minimized by, \begin{equation}J_Ising<0 ~(FM)~: ~f_1= 2\theta_1~~,~~ J_Ising>0 ~(AFM)~: ~f_1= 2\theta_1+\pi J_Kitaev>0 ~(AFM)~: ~f_1= 2\theta_1~~,~~ J_Kitaev<0 ~(FM)~: ~f_1= 2\theta_1+\pi\end{equation} The Ising and Kitaev term give rise to opposite kind of canting.
elements.sent-2411-236: Note that the $U(1)$ symmetry breaking decides the canting angle of the spin, hence the net canting moment on the triangle.
elements.sent-2411-237: The net canted moment on the triangle is given by, \begin{equation}m=\Bigg\{{c} \frac{1}{2}\left\{\cos(\theta_1~,~-\sin(\theta_1))\right\}~,~~for FM Ising and AFM Kitaev \frac{1}{2}\left\{-\cos(\theta_1)~,~\sin(\theta_1))\right\}~,~~for AFM Ising and FM Kitaev \end{equation} We can parameterize the canted moment as, \begin{equation}m = \frac{1}{2}\left\{\cos(\gamma)~,~\sin(\gamma)\right\}\end{equation} Then we find that for AFM (FM) bond-Ising (Kitaev), $\gamma = - \theta_1$ and AFM (FM) bond-Ising (Kitaev) gives time-reversed canted moments, $\gamma = - \theta_1 + \pi$.}
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation}m=\Bigg\{{c} \frac{1}{2}\left\{\cos(\theta_1~,~-\sin(\theta_1))\right\}~,~~for FM Ising and AFM Kitaev \frac{1}{2}\left\{-\cos(\theta_1)~,~\sin(\theta_1))\right\}~,~~for AFM Ising and FM Kitaev }\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-238: Note that $\theta_1$ is the global choice of the antichiral order which breaks the $U(1)$ symmetry of the Heisenberg model and DM interaction.
elements.sent-2411-239: In Fig. \ref{fig_canting_ising , we plot the net FM moments due to the bond-Ising and Kitaev term (both FM and AFM) on a star of David of the kagome lattice for different choices of $\theta_1$.}
elements.sent-2411-240: Note that although both terms generate similar canting, the magnitude of their effect depends on the coupling constant of these two terms.
elements.sent-2411-241: Generically, for the kagome lattice with DM anisotropy, it is expected that Kitaev interaction is more dominant than the other anisotropic exchanges.
elements.sent-2411-242: Therefore, in the main text, we only discuss the Kitaev interaction leaving aside the contribution from bond-Ising term.

elements.section-2411-008: Canting due to strong bond disorder
elements.sent-2411-243: In this section, we describe the local canting due to ABH disorder from the ${\bf q =0$ single triangle classical analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-244: Note that, depending on the density of the ABH, there can be two different disordered triangles possible: triangles with one of the bond either stronger or weaker than the other two (occurs even for a single isolated ABH), and triangles with all the three bonds are of different strength (occurs only for adjacent ABH).
elements.sent-2411-245: The spin Hamiltonians for these two cases are given by: \begin{equation}H_{ABH,1}=H_{Heisenberg}+H_DMz+\Delta J S_2\cdot S_3 H_{ABH,2}=H_{Heisenberg}+H_DMz+\Delta J\left( S_2\cdot S_3- S_1\cdot S_2\right)\end{equation} For reference of the labels of the spins, see Fig. \ref{fig_reference .}
elements.formula-2411-019: \begin{equation}H_{ABH,1}=H_{Heisenberg}+H_DMz+\Delta J S_2\cdot S_3 H_{ABH,2}=H_{Heisenberg}+H_DMz+\Delta J\left( S_2\cdot S_3- S_1\cdot S_2\right)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-246: We note that both of the above Hamiltonian do not break global $U(1)$ invariance.
elements.sent-2411-247: Hence we measure the angle of $S_2$ and $S_3$ from $S_1$.
elements.sent-2411-248: The antichiral AFM order (atleast short ranged) is established by the combination of $H_\text{Heisenberg}$ and $H_\text{DMz}$, before the onset of the FM canting.
elements.sent-2411-249: Therefore, we treat the disorder term perturbatively around the antichiral states.
elements.sent-2411-250: We assume: \begin{equation}\theta_2=\frac{4\pi}{3}+\delta_{ABH},~~ \theta_3=\frac{2\pi}{3}-\delta_{ABH},\end{equation} where $\delta_{ABH}$ denotes the amount of FM canting due to ABH disorder(see Fig. \ref{fig_reference ).}
elements.sent-2411-251: Substituting the above in the classical energy expression, and solving their minima, we obtain:.
elements.sent-2411-252: For $H_{ABH,1}$ (isolated ABH): \begin{equation}\delta_{ABH,1}=\Bigg\{{lr} -\frac{\pi}{3} ~~for ~~\Delta J<-0.5 i\left(\log(4)-\log\left(\frac{(i+\sqrt{3})\left(-i-i\Delta J+\sqrt{(1+\Delta J)^2(3+4\Delta J(2+\Delta J))}\right)}{2(1+\Delta J)^2}\right)\right) ~~for ~~\Delta J>-0.5 \end{equation} The net moment of the triangle can be computed as: \begin{equation}m_{ABH,1}=1+2\cos\left(\frac{4\pi}{3}+\delta_{ABH,1}\right)=\Bigg\{{lr} -1 ~~for ~~\Delta J<-0.5 \frac{\Delta J}{1+\Delta J} ~~for ~~\Delta J>-0.5 \end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-021: \begin{equation}\delta_{ABH,1}=\Bigg\{{lr} -\frac{\pi}{3} ~~for ~~\Delta J<-0.5 i\left(\log(4)-\log\left(\frac{(i+\sqrt{3})\left(-i-i\Delta J+\sqrt{(1+\Delta J)^2(3+4\Delta J(2+\Delta J))}\right)}{2(1+\Delta J)^2}\right)\right) ~~for ~~\Delta J>-0.5 }\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-253: For $H_{ABH,2}$ (adjacent ABH): \begin{equation}\delta_{ABH,2}=\Bigg\{{lr} -\frac{\pi}{3} ~~for ~~\Delta J<-0.4 \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}\left(-2+\Delta J(\Delta J-1)+\sqrt{(1+\Delta J)^2(2+\Delta J)(2+5\Delta J)}\right)}{\left(2+\Delta J-\Delta J^2+3\sqrt{(\Delta J+1)^2(\Delta J+2)(5\Delta J+2)}\right)}\right) ~~for ~~\Delta J>-0.4 \end{equation} The net moment of the triangle is given by: \begin{equation}m_{ABH,2}=1+2\cos\left(\frac{4\pi}{3}+\delta_{ABH,2}\right)=\Bigg\{{lr} -1 ~~for ~~\Delta J<-0.4 \frac{3\Delta J}{2+2\Delta J} ~~for ~~\Delta J>-0.4 \end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-023: \begin{equation}\delta_{ABH,2}=\Bigg\{{lr} -\frac{\pi}{3} ~~for ~~\Delta J<-0.4 \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}\left(-2+\Delta J(\Delta J-1)+\sqrt{(1+\Delta J)^2(2+\Delta J)(2+5\Delta J)}\right)}{\left(2+\Delta J-\Delta J^2+3\sqrt{(\Delta J+1)^2(\Delta J+2)(5\Delta J+2)}\right)}\right) ~~for ~~\Delta J>-0.4 }\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-254: Note that the qualitative results obtained in the above two cases are very similar.
elements.sent-2411-255: The difference lies in the detailed values of the canted moment.
elements.sent-2411-256: In Fig. \ref{fig_net moment_ABH_single triangle classical , we plot the net FM moment of the triangle for the both the kinds of disorder.}
elements.sent-2411-257: In Fig. \ref{fig_canting_ABH , we show the FM canting of the triangles surrounding the ABH (star of David).}
elements.sent-2411-258: Note that due to a $C_3$ symmetry of the ABH hexagon, the canted configuration also has a $C_3$ rotation symmetry, leading to zero net moment on each such hexagons.
elements.sent-2411-259: Interestingly, the canted moments due to ABH forms a vortex configuration with voricity -1, which is a consequence of the underlying antichiral pattern.

elements.sent-2411-260: The search for new quantum spin liquid materials relies on systems with strong frustration such as spins on an ideal kagome lattice.
elements.sent-2411-261: However, lattice imperfections can have substantial effects which are as yet not well understood.
elements.sent-2411-262: In recent work the 2D kagome system YCu$_3$(OH)$_6$[(Cl$_x$Br$_{(1-x)}$)$_{3-y}$(OH)$_y$] (YCOB-Cl) has emerged as a leading candidate hosting a Dirac spin liquid which appears to survive at least for $x<0.4$, associated with alternating-bond hexagon (ABH) disorder.
elements.sent-2411-263: Here in samples with $x=0.58, y=0.1$ we report unusual in-plane ferromagnetic canting (FM) of the in-plane antiferromagnet with moreover an expanded regime of short-ranged order, and propose a theoretical model to explain this behavior.
elements.sent-2411-264: We show that Kitaev type exchanges naturally arise on the kagome lattice to second order in the known Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya exchanges, and that these interactions can produce the unusual in-plane FM moments.
elements.sent-2411-265: We propose a spin cluster phenomenological model to describe the short-ranged regime and analyze quantum fluctuations in a toy model to show how ABH disorder helps stabilize this regime.
elements.sent-2411-266: The combination of experimental observation and theory suggests that kagome-Kitaev interactions and ABH disorder are necessary for describing the magnetic fluctuations in this family of materials, with potential implications for the proposed proximate spin liquid phase.
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{"Figure_1".png} \caption{\label{fig1} The temperature dependence of the magnetization, $M$, for the 58\% chlorine doped sample under FC and ZFC conditions (main panel) under a field of 50 Oe in the kagome plane (perpendicular to $c$). elements.sent-2411-267: In this joint theory-experiment work we begin (Sec.\ \ref{sec_exp ) by presenting comprehensive measurements on samples with $x=0.58$ and $y=0.1$.}
elements.sent-2411-268: The Cl concentration is large enough such that an ordered state is observed \cite{Xu2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-269: However, the magnetic order is quite unusual.
elements.sent-2411-270: With decreasing temperature there is at first a onset of short-ranged AFM order around $T_*\sim 14 $ K.
elements.sent-2411-271: Further decreasing the temperature we observe a small ferromagnetic (FM) component to develop, which is primarily in-plane.
elements.sent-2411-272: Long range order, including hysteresis of the FM component, finally sets in below $T_\text{L =7 $ K.}
elements.sent-2411-273: The unusual anisotropy of the FM canting and the extended regime of short-ranged order cannot be described by the usual geometric frustration or magnetic impurities on the kagome lattice.
elements.sent-2411-274: We theoretically approach this puzzle by considering each ingredient separately.
elements.sent-2411-275: To account for in-plane ferromagnetism we propose additional unconventional interactions which are a kagome variant of the Kitaev exchanges.
elements.sent-2411-276: We show these are symmetry allowed and moreover microscopically expected due to the presence of already known DM interactions.
elements.sent-2411-277: We show that in-plane ferromagnetism is produced by such small Kitaev-like bond dependent Ising interactions.
elements.sent-2411-278: Given this theoretical mechanism for in-plane canting, we propose a phenomenological spin cluster model to describe the unusual magnetism observed in the intermediate temperature regime of $T_L<T<T_*$.
elements.sent-2411-279: To identify a possible origin of the spin clusters and the associated expanded short ranged order regime, we then turn to the ABH defects.
elements.sent-2411-280: Using both quantum and classical analysis we show that the ABH disorder stretches the stability of the short-ranged order via both nucleation of the local order parameter around defects as well as by enhancing the quantum fluctuations that suppress a classically ordered state.
elements.sent-2411-281: The ABH disorder and resulting spin clusters, together with the weak but consequential kagome-Kitaev exchanges, can thus account for the unusual puzzle seen in the magnetism of the ordered compound.
elements.sent-2411-282: The rest of the manuscript is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-283: In Sec.\ \ref{sec_exp , we give the details of the experimental observations.}
elements.sent-2411-284: In Sec.\ \ref{sec_antichiral we give the minimal model for the antichiral AFM order, containing Heisenberg and Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya terms, and derive the terms that then necessarily appear at higher order in the spin orbit coupling, including the kagome Kitaev terms.}
elements.sent-2411-285: In Sec.\ \ref{sec_kitaev , we give the details of the Kitaev term and derive the associated in-plane canting via a classical $q=0$ analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-286: Sec.\ \ref{sec_cluster describes the phenomenology of the FM cluster model and its connection to the experimental observations.}
elements.sent-2411-287: Finally, in Sec.\ \ref{sec_ABH , we describe the role of ABH disorder on the magnetic order and indicate the mechanism of obtaining an extended regime of short-ranged order.}
elements.sent-2411-288: The details of various calculations are further given in the appendices.
elements.sent-2411-289: Experimental findings: in-plane ferromagnetic canting and extended regime of short ranged order.

elements.sent-2411-301: Minimal spin model for antichiral AFM order with in-plane canting In this section, we first present an argument to the readers that experimentally observed magnetic order is antichiral antiferromagnetism along with a small in-plane ferromagnetic component.
elements.sent-2411-302: We then give a microscopic mechanism of how to obtain the in-plane ferromagnetism which cannot be described via the conventional interactions on the kagome lattice.
elements.sent-2411-303: The most dominant symmetry allowed spin exchange Hamiltonian on ideal single layer kagome lattice can be written as~\cite{ElhajalPRB2002 \begin{equation}H=J\sum_{\langle ij\rangle} S_i\cdot S_j+D_z \sum_{\langle ij\rangle }\left(S_i\times S_j\right)_z+\cdots, \label{eq_heis_dm}\end{equation} where the first and the second term denote the isotropic Heisenberg and anisotropic DM interaction with DM vector being perpendicular to the kagome plane (see Fig. \ref{fig_antichiral (a)), respectively, and ``$\cdots$" denote other symmetry allowed couplings which will be discussed later.}}
elements.sent-2411-304: In this family of materials, the DM term is typically estimated to be $\mathcal{O}\left(10^{-2}\right)$ smaller than the Heisenberg interaction~\cite{lee2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-305: In other words, if spin and space were unphysically decoupled, it would have rotation symmetries which act oppositely on spin and on space.
elements.sent-2411-306: The consideration of the crystal field environment around the magnetic sites generically breaks the mirror symmetry about the pure kagome plane, and allows the in-plane DM terms (perpendicular to the kagome bonds, see Fig. \ref{fig_antichiral (a)) in the above Hamiltonian.}
elements.sent-2411-307: For the chiral states, the in-plane terms generate an out-of-plane canting of the spins leading to a net FM moment perpendicular to the kagome plane.
elements.sent-2411-308: Thus there is no energy gain due to the DM term for canting and the cost due to the Heisenberg exchange forbids it.
elements.sent-2411-309: Given the DM vector on a bond connecting site $i$ and $j$ being ${ \hat{D _{ij}$, the symmetric easy axis anisotropic interaction is given by, \begin{equation}H_soc, sym= \frac{1}{J}\left({ S_i}\cdot{\hat{D}_{ij}}\right) \left( { S_j}\cdot { \hat{D}_{ji}}\right).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-310: Clearly, such term varies quadratically with the spin-orbit coupling and remains weak compared to other exchanges described previously.
elements.sent-2411-311: Since the net DM vector in kagome is a linear combination of out-of-plane (perpendicular to kagome plane) and in-plane (perpendicular to the kagome bond) component (see Fig. \ref{fig_antichiral (a)), it leads to following decomposition of the anisotropic exchange interaction: \begin{equation}H_soc, sym=\alpha_1 H_{c-Ising }+\alpha_2 H_Kitaev+\alpha_3 H_cross,\end{equation} where $\alpha_i$s are constant coefficients which depend on the ratio of the in-plane and out-of-plane DM component.}
elements.sent-2411-312: In the above decomposition, $H_{c\text{-Ising }\propto \sum_{\langle ij\rangle S^c_iS^c_j$ denotes the Ising interaction with easy axis perpendicular to the kagome plane ($c-$axis).}}
elements.sent-2411-313: Explicit expressions are given in the Appendix \ref{appen_kitaev_ising .}
elements.sent-2411-314: We note that $c-$axis Ising term cannot generate the in-plane ferromagnetic canting, whereas $H_\text{cross}$ gives the in-plane canting only to the subleading order.
elements.sent-2411-315: Therefore, the dominant in-plane canting arises from the Kitaev term which we explicitly compute in the following section.
elements.sent-2411-316: We note that in other kagome systems there could be other symmetry allowed bond dependent Ising interactions with easy axes lying in the plane, that also can potentially give rise to the in-plane FM canting.
elements.sent-2411-317: One of such example is the bond-Ising interaction.
elements.sent-2411-318: For completeness we discuss canting effects from the bond-Ising term in the Appendix \ref{appen_kitaev_ising .}
elements.sent-2411-319: We now proceed to focus on the Kitaev-type interaction to show that it does indeed produce canting.

elements.sent-2411-331: Having discussed the origin of the small ferromagnetism, we now focus on the second part of the observed puzzle of whether there is extended regime of short-ranged order below $T_*$ before the onset of true long-ranged magnetism.
elements.sent-2411-332: We answer this question in two parts.
elements.sent-2411-333: First, we phenomenologically describe a spin cluster model, and show that the short-ranged clusters along with their in-plane moments due to Kitaev interaction describes the experimental observations quite well.
elements.sent-2411-334: In the second part, we give a more microscopic understanding of our phenomenological model by indicating that the interesting interplay of disorder and antichiral AFM order can potentially lead to enhanced stability of such short-ranged clusters.
elements.sent-2411-335: In this section we focus on the first part of our theoretical modeling.
elements.sent-2411-336: Phenomenologically, we attribute the $T_*$ temperature to the onset of a short-ranged correlated phase, where the magnetism is controlled via independent finite spin clusters.
elements.sent-2411-337: Such clusters gain a small in-plane ferromagnetic moment as we lower the temperature.
elements.sent-2411-338: The magnetization of the clusters can then be written as: \begin{equation}M = n_c(T)gS_eff(T)\mu_BL\left(\frac{g\mu_BBS_eff(T)}{k_BT}\right) + \chi_AF(T)B\end{equation} In this equation the magnetic response is split into two components: the first term corresponds to the response of the free spin clusters, whereas the second describes the response of the AFM.
elements.sent-2411-339: In the above equation, the free spin magnetization is approximated by the Langevin function $L(x)=coth(x)-1/x$ which is the large spin ($S_\text{eff}$) limit of the Brillouin function.
elements.sent-2411-340: In Fig. \ref{fig4 , we plot the parameters $n_c$ (closed circles), and $S_\text{eff}$ (open circles) obtained from fits to the $M$ vs $B$ isotherms at each temperature.}
elements.sent-2411-341: Interestingly, $\chi_\text{AF}$ shows a peak (bottom panel) as expected for an AFM transition, though the peak position extracted from this few-parameter $M(B)$ fit appears to be shifted from the true AFM transition.
elements.sent-2411-342: Interestingly, the product $n_c N_c \alpha_m = n_c S_\text{eff}$ shows a strikingly simple temperature dependence (Figure 4 middle panel).

elements.sent-2411-344: Extended regime of short-ranged order: Role of Alternating-Bond-Hexagon (ABH) disorder In this section, we will look for a mechanism of obtaining the spin clusters based on the ABH disorder present in the current material.

elements.sent-2411-355: The kagome lattice Heisenberg model is geometrically frustrated, with various possibilities of competing orders and frustration due to the competing interactions.
elements.sent-2411-356: The frustration can be seen already for classical Heisenberg interactions.
elements.sent-2411-357: Beyond the Heisenberg exchanges, the small DM coupling in addition to the Heisenberg interaction favors the antichiral states.
elements.sent-2411-359: But the antichiral state is still geometrically frustrated, akin to other 120 degree states on lattices with triangles.
elements.sent-2411-360: The ABH, incorporating the stronger and weaker couplings, partially relieves the frustration locally near the defect.
elements.sent-2411-361: It modifies the classical energy (per bond) of the classical antichiral order from $J/2$ to $J/2 - (\Delta J)^2/6J$.
elements.sent-2411-362: This local canting arising from ABH gives no net coarse-grained FM moment, but rather a larger-scale AFM pattern (see appendices for details).
elements.sent-2411-363: The local reduction of frustration around the ABH can potentially help to set in short-ranged ordering on the ABH more easily than the rest of the pristine kagome, leading to the cluster model which has been introduced phenomenologically in the earlier sections.
elements.sent-2411-364: Phenomenologically, this enhanced $|S|_\text{avg}$ can be interpreted as a local enhancement of short-ranged order near each ABH.
elements.sent-2411-365: An interesting corollary of the above effect can be the pinning of density of the short-ranged cluster to that of the ABH, which may be assessed in future experiments that can study the regime of short ranged clusters while characterizing the ABH defects.
elements.sent-2411-366: Phenomenologically, this enhanced quantum fluctuation can result in suppression of long-range classical order and enhanced regime of short-ranged order arising from ABH.
elements.sent-2411-367: Such effects are especially quite significant near the $T_* \sim 14$ K, where the short-ranged order is weak enough.
elements.sent-2411-368: At much lower temperatures, the mean field due to the short-ranged domains enhances, leading to long-range ordered phase at $T_L \sim 7$ K.
elements.sent-2411-369: In the regime of disordered ABH density where there is an AFM ordered state, we find evidence for the formation of FM clusters.
elements.sent-2411-370: It would be interesting to consider how increasing ABH disorder may interplay with a phase transition into a Dirac QSL, and what the role of ferromagnetic spin clusters may be across such a transition.

elements.sent-2411-395: Lack of in-plane canting for chiral state Here we give additional details for why the chiral (as opposed to antichiral) state does not get in-plane canting.
elements.sent-2411-396: First recall the discussion from the main text.
elements.sent-2411-397: The DM interaction appears in the first order of spin-orbit coupling ($\lambda_{SOC}$), whereas the Kitaev and bond-Ising terms appear in the second order in $\lambda_{SOC}$.
elements.sent-2411-398: So DM interactions whether in-plane or out-of-plane are larger than the anisotropic Ising interactions.
elements.sent-2411-399: Therefore, if there was a chiral order in the material, the symmetry allowed in-plane already gives out-of-plane ferromagnetism, which is not observed in the experiments.
elements.sent-2411-400: This clearly rules out the possibility of chiral order, as opposed to antichiral order.
elements.sent-2411-401: The in plane FM order requires antichiral order, and an additional ingredient beyond DM.
elements.sent-2411-402: Now keeping the above in mind we can separately ask what does the in plane anisotropy such as Ising terms do to the chiral order.
elements.sent-2411-403: The main difference in the chiral case compared to the antichiral is there is no emergent $U(1)$ symmetry in the chiral subspace for the bond-Ising or Kitaev interaction, therefore a chiral state is chosen by the anisotropic interactions.
elements.sent-2411-404: If we do the perturbation analysis of energy gained by canting about this state, $\phi_2 \rightarrow \frac{2 \pi {3 + \phi_1 + \frac{\left(b + a/\sqrt{3}\right) {2 , \phi_3 \rightarrow \frac{4 \pi {3 + \phi_1 + \frac{\left( b - a/\sqrt{3}\right) {2}$ , we obtain: \begin{equation}\left(\frac{H_Ising}{J_{Ising}}\right)_chiral \approx-\frac{3}{4} \left(1 + 2 \cos\left(2 \phi_1\right)\right) + b \sin\left(2 \phi_1\right)+... \left(\frac{H_Kitaev}{J_Kitaev}\right)_chiral \approx -\frac{1}{2} - \cos\left(2 \phi_1\right) + \frac{2}{3} b \sin\left(2 \phi_1\right)+...\end{equation}.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-405: The minima is obtained by setting $\phi_1=0$.
elements.sent-2411-406: Clearly, the the energy gain due to canting is zero to the first order in perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-407: Hence the chiral order does not get any in-plane canting to the leading order.
